title: Calculating a t interval for a mean
description: Practice constructing a one-sample t interval for a mean.
topic_path: Calculating a t interval for a mean

assessment_items.question_content:
A nutritionist wants to estimate the average caloric content of the burritos at a popular restaurant. They obtain a random sample of $14$ burritos and measure their caloric content. Their sample data are roughly symmetric with a mean of $700$ calories and a standard deviation of $50$ calories. 

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $95\%$ confidence interval for the mean caloric content of these burritos?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Conditions and formula 

Note that we have met all of the conditions for making a $t$ interval to estimate the mean.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Here's the formula for a one-sample $t$ interval for a mean:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm \left({\text{critical}\atop\text{value}}\right)\left({\text{standard deviation} \atop\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\bar x &\pm\ t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n} 
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=700$.

Our sample size is $n=14$.

Our sample standard deviation is $s_x=50$.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=14-1=13$, so for $95\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=2.16$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Computation

$\begin{align}
\bar x &\pm t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n}  \\\\
700 &\pm 2.16 \cdot \dfrac{50}{\sqrt {14}} \\\\
700 &\pm (2.16)(13.363) \\\\
700 &\pm 28.864
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A school psychologist at a large high school took a random sample of $16$ students and asked them how much sleep they get on a typical weekend night. The sample data were roughly symmetric with a mean of $8.75$ hours and a standard deviation of $1.5$ hours. 

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $90\%$ confidence interval for the mean amount of sleep (in hours) this population of students gets on a typical weekend night?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Conditions and formula 

Note that we have met all of the conditions for making a $t$ interval to estimate the mean.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Here's the formula for a one-sample $t$ interval for a mean:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm \left({\text{critical}\atop\text{value}}\right)\left({\text{standard deviation} \atop\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\bar x &\pm\ t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n} 
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=8.25$.

Our sample size is $n=16$.

Our sample standard deviation is $s_x=1.5$.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=16-1=15$, so for $90\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=1.753$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Computation

$\begin{align}
\bar x &\pm t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n}  \\\\
8.75 &\pm 1.753 \cdot \dfrac{1.5}{\sqrt {16}} \\\\
8.75 &\pm (1.753)(0.375) \\\\
8.75 &\pm 0.657
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A quality control expert at a drink bottling factory took a random sample of $9$ bottles from a production run of over $1{,}000$ bottles and measured the amount of liquid in each bottle in the sample. The sample data were roughly symmetric with a mean of $505\,\text{mL}$ and a standard deviation of $6\,\text{mL}$. 

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $95\%$ confidence interval for the mean amount of liquid per bottle (in $\text{mL}$) in this production run?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Conditions and formula 

Note that we have met all of the conditions for making a $t$ interval to estimate the mean.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Here's the formula for a one-sample $t$ interval for a mean:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm \left({\text{critical}\atop\text{value}}\right)\left({\text{standard deviation} \atop\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\bar x &\pm\ t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n} 
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=505$.

Our sample size is $n=9$.

Our sample standard deviation is $s_x=6$.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=9-1=8$, so for $95\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=2.306$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Computation

$\begin{align}
\bar x &\pm t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n}  \\\\
505 &\pm 2.306 \cdot \dfrac{6}{\sqrt {9}} \\\\
505 &\pm (2.306)(2) \\\\
505 &\pm 4.612
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A quality control expert at a pretzel factory took a random sample of $10$ bags from a production run of over $500$ bags and measured the amount of pretzels in each bag in the sample. The sample data were roughly symmetric with a mean of $450\,\text{g}$ and a standard deviation of $15\,\text{g}$. 

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $90\%$ confidence interval for the mean amount of pretzels per bag (in grams) in this production run?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Conditions and formula 

Note that we have met all of the conditions for making a $t$ interval to estimate the mean.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Here's the formula for a one-sample $t$ interval for a mean:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm \left({\text{critical}\atop\text{value}}\right)\left({\text{standard deviation} \atop\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\bar x &\pm\ t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n} 
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=450$.

Our sample size is $n=10$.

Our sample standard deviation is $s_x=15$.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=10-1=9$, so for $90\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=1.833$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Computation

$\begin{align}
\bar x &\pm t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n}  \\\\
450 &\pm 1.833 \cdot \dfrac{15}{\sqrt {10}} \\\\
450 &\pm (1.833)(4.473) \\\\
450 &\pm 8.695
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
An economist studying fuel costs wants to estimate the mean price of gasoline in her state on a certain day. On that day, she takes an [[☃ definition 1]] of $12$ gas stations and finds the sample mean price (in dollars per gallon) is $\bar x=\$2.29$ with a standard deviation of $s_x=\$0.20$. The prices in the sample are roughly symmetric with no distinct outliers.

**Based on this sample, which of the following is closest to a $90\%$ confidence interval for the mean price of gasoline that day in her state?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Conditions and formula 

Note that we have met all of the conditions for making a $t$ interval to estimate the mean.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Here's the formula for a one-sample $t$ interval for a mean:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm \left({\text{critical}\atop\text{value}}\right)\left({\text{standard deviation} \atop\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\bar x &\pm\ t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n} 
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=\$2.29$.

Our sample size is $n=12$.

Our sample standard deviation is $s_x=\$0.20$.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=12-1=11$, so for $90\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=1.796$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.definition: A simple random sample (SRS) is a sample taken so that each member and set of $n$ members has an equal chance of being in the sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
A safety auditor at a paint factory took a random sample of $25$ cans of paint from a production run of over $1{,}000$ cans and measured the amount of lead in each can of paint in the sample. The sample data were roughly symmetric with a mean of $37$ parts per million (ppm) of lead per can and a standard deviation of $18\,\text{ppm}$.

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $95\%$ confidence interval for the mean amount of lead per can of paint (in ppm) in this production run?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Conditions and formula 

Note that we have met all of the conditions for making a $t$ interval to estimate the mean.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Here's the formula for a one-sample $t$ interval for a mean:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm \left({\text{critical}\atop\text{value}}\right)\left({\text{standard deviation} \atop\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\bar x &\pm\ t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n} 
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=37$.

Our sample size is $n=25$.

Our sample standard deviation is $s_x=18$.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=25-1=24$, so for $95\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=2.064$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Ignacio is curious about the average age of cars in the commuter lot at his large university. He takes a random sample of $16$ cars and finds that their average age is $\bar x=14.5$ years and their standard deviation is $s_x=4.6$ years. The distribution of ages in the sample was roughly symmetric with no obvious outliers.

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $90\%$ confidence interval for the mean age of cars (in years) in this commuter lot?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Conditions and formula 

Note that we have met all of the conditions for making a $t$ interval to estimate the mean.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Here's the formula for a one-sample $t$ interval for a mean:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm \left({\text{critical}\atop\text{value}}\right)\left({\text{standard deviation} \atop\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\bar x &\pm\ t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n} 
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=14.5$.

Our sample size is $n=16$.

Our sample standard deviation is $s_x=4.6$.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=16-1=15$, so for $90\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=1.753$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Camila is curious about the average length of the female blue whales her organization monitors. She takes a random sample of $4$ female blue whales from this population. Their average recorded length is $\bar x=78\,\text{m}$ and their standard deviation is $s_x=10\,\text{m}$. The distribution of lengths in the sample is roughly symmetric with no obvious outliers.

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $95\%$ confidence interval for the mean length (in meters) of female blue whales?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Conditions and formula 

Note that we have met all of the conditions for making a $t$ interval to estimate the mean.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Here's the formula for a one-sample $t$ interval for a mean:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm \left({\text{critical}\atop\text{value}}\right)\left({\text{standard deviation} \atop\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\bar x &\pm\ t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n} 
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=78$.

Our sample size is $n=4$.

Our sample standard deviation is $s_x=10$.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=4-1=3$, so for $95\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=3.182$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Marcelina has over $500$ songs on her mobile phone, and she wants to estimate the average length of the songs (in minutes). She takes an [[☃ definition 1]] of $28$ songs on her phone and calculates a sample mean of $3.4$ minutes and a standard deviation of $0.72$ minutes. The song lengths in the sample were roughly symmetric with no clear outliers.

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $99\%$ confidence interval for the mean length (in minutes) of the songs on her phone?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Conditions and formula 

Note that we have met all of the conditions for making a $t$ interval to estimate the mean.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Here's the formula for a one-sample $t$ interval for a mean:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm \left({\text{critical}\atop\text{value}}\right)\left({\text{standard deviation} \atop\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\bar x &\pm\ t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n} 
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=3.4$ minutes.

Our sample size is $n=28$ songs.

Our sample standard deviation is $s_x=0.72$ minutes.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=28-1=27$, so for $99\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=2.77$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Answer

$3.4 \pm 2.77\left(\dfrac{0.72}{\sqrt{28}}\right)$

options.definition: A simple random sample (SRS) is a sample taken so that each member and set of $n$ members has an equal chance of being in the sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
Caviar is an expensive delicacy, so companies that package it pay very close attention to the amount of product in their tins. Suppose a company that produces over $1{,}000$ tins of caviar per day took an [[☃ definition 1]] of $20$ tins from one day's production. The sample showed a mean of $99.8\,\text{g}$ of caviar per tin with a standard deviation of $0.9\,\text{g}$. The amounts were roughly symmetric with no outliers.

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $95\%$ confidence interval for the mean amount of caviar (in grams) per tin from that day's production?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Conditions and formula 

Note that we have met all of the conditions for making a $t$ interval to estimate the mean.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Here's the formula for a one-sample $t$ interval for a mean:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm \left({\text{critical}\atop\text{value}}\right)\left({\text{standard deviation} \atop\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\bar x &\pm\ t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n} 
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=99.8\,\text{g}$

Our sample size is $n=20$.

Our sample standard deviation is $s_x=0.9\,\text{g}$.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=20-1=19$, so for $95\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=2.093$.

[[☃ explanation 3]]

###Answer

$99.8 \pm 2.093\left(\dfrac{0.9}{\sqrt{20}}\right)$

options.definition: A simple random sample (SRS) is a sample taken so that each member and set of $n$ members has an equal chance of being in the sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
Fiona grows large pumpkins and wants to estimate the mean weight of the approximately $500$ pumpkins in her harvest. She takes a random sample of $11$ of these pumpkins and finds they have a mean weight of $44\,\text{kg}$ and a standard deviation of $6.7\,\text{kg}$. The weights in the sample were roughly symmetric with no outliers.

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $99\%$ confidence interval for the mean weight (in $\text{kg}$) of pumpkins in this harvest?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Conditions and formula 

Note that we have met all of the conditions for making a $t$ interval to estimate the mean.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Here's the formula for a one-sample $t$ interval for a mean:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm \left({\text{critical}\atop\text{value}}\right)\left({\text{standard deviation} \atop\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\bar x &\pm\ t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n} 
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=44\,\text{kg}$

Our sample size is $n=11$.

Our sample standard deviation is $s_x=6.7\,\text{kg}$.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=11-1=10$, so for $99\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=3.169$.

[[☃ explanation 4]]

###Answer

$44 \pm 3.169\left(\dfrac{6.7}{\sqrt{11}}\right)$

assessment_items.question_content:
A pharmaceutical company produces caffeine pills that are each supposed to contain $200\,\text{mg}$ of caffeine. A plant that produces thousands of these pills per batch took an [[☃ definition 1]] of $15$ pills from their latest batch to see if they contained the proper amount of caffeine. The sample data had a mean of $200.4\,\text{mg}$ of caffeine per pill with a standard deviation of $0.8\,\text{mg}$. The amounts were roughly symmetric with no outliers.

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $95\%$ confidence interval for the mean amount of caffeine (in $\text{mg}$) per pill in this batch?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###Conditions and formula 

Note that we have met all of the conditions for making a $t$ interval to estimate the mean.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Here's the formula for a one-sample $t$ interval for a mean:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm \left({\text{critical}\atop\text{value}}\right)\left({\text{standard deviation} \atop\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\bar x &\pm\ t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n} 
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=200.4\,\text{mg}$

Our sample size is $n=15$.

Our sample standard deviation is $s_x=0.8\,\text{mg}$.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=15-1=14$, so for $95\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=2.145$.

[[☃ explanation 3]]

###Answer

$200.4 \pm 2.145\left(\dfrac{0.8}{\sqrt{15}}\right)$

options.definition: A simple random sample (SRS) is a sample taken so that each member and set of $n$ members has an equal chance of being in the sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
Carlos is interested in estimating the mean amount of time it takes players to complete a level of the game he's designing. He recruits $7$ players of varying skill levels and ages to play the level until they complete it (he's willing to treat this as a random sample), and he times how long it takes each of them. They play separately from each other, so Carlos is also willing to assume independence. Here are the data with summary statistics:

Player | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ | $4$ | $5$ | $6$ | $7$ 
-: | -: | -: | -: | -: | -: | -: | -:
**Time (minutes)** | $48$ | $46$ | $40$ | $34$ | $49$ | $40$ | $37$
**Mean** | $\bar x=42\,\text{min}$ |  |  |  |  |  | 
**Standard deviation** | $s_x=5.7\,\text{min}$ |  |  |  |  |  | 

Assume that all conditions for inference are met for this sample.

**Which of the following is a $95\%$ confidence interval for the mean time (in minutes) it takes players to complete this level?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Conditions and formula 

The problem said to assume that all conditions for inference are met (which they are), so we'll make a $t$ interval to estimate the mean.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Here's the formula for a one-sample $t$ interval for a mean:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm \left({\text{critical}\atop\text{value}}\right)\left({\text{standard deviation} \atop\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\bar x &\pm\ t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n} 
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=42$ minutes.

Our sample size is $n=7$ players.

Our sample standard deviation is $s_x=5.7$ minutes.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=7-1=6$, so for $95\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=2.447$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Computation

$\begin{align}
\bar x &\pm t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n}  \\\\
42 &\pm 2.447 \cdot \dfrac{5.7}{\sqrt {7}} \\\\
42 &\pm (2.447)(2.154) \\\\
42 &\pm 5.27
\end{align}$

###Answer

$42 \pm 5.3$

assessment_items.question_content:
Olivia works at a company that creates mobile phones. She wanted to estimate the mean amount of time their new phone's battery lasts with regular use after a full charge. She took a random sample of $6$ of these phones and randomly assigned each of them to a volunteer. She instructed them to fully charge the phones and use them as they regularly would until the battery died (without recharging the phone). Here are the data they reported:

Phone | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ | $4$ | $5$ | $6$
-: | -: | -: | -: | -: | -: | -: 
**Battery life (hours)** | $8.0$ | $6.0$ | $10.5$ | $9.0$ | $8.5$ | $12$ 
**Mean** | $\bar x=9$ hours |  |  |  |  |  
**Standard deviation** | $s_x=2.07$ hours |  |  |  |  |  

Assume that all conditions for inference are met.

**Which of the following is a $90\%$ confidence interval for the mean battery life (in hours)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Conditions and formula 

The problem said to assume that all conditions for inference are met, so we'll make a $t$ interval to estimate the mean.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Here's the formula for a one-sample $t$ interval for a mean:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm \left({\text{critical}\atop\text{value}}\right)\left({\text{standard deviation} \atop\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\bar x &\pm\ t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n} 
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=9$ hours.

Our sample size is $n=6$ phones.

Our sample standard deviation is $s_x=2.07$ hours.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=6-1=5$, so for $90\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=2.015$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Computation

$\begin{align}
\bar x &\pm t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n}  \\\\
9 &\pm 2.015 \cdot \dfrac{2.07}{\sqrt {6}} \\\\
9 &\pm (2.015)(0.845) \\\\
9 &\pm 1.703
\end{align}$

###Answer

$9 \pm 1.7$

assessment_items.question_content:
A company wanted to estimate the mean lifetime of its new model of lightbulbs. They use a method for testing bulbs that accelerates the process so the bulbs burn out relatively quickly, and the company can accurately calculate the corresponding lifetime under regular usage. They took a random sample of $5$ of these new bulbs and calculated their lifetimes. Here are the data and summary statistics:

Bulb | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ | $4$ | $5$
-: | -: | -: | -: | -: | -: 
**Lifetime (years)** | $14.2$ | $12.2$ | $13.4$ | $12.6$ | $14.6$ 
**Mean** | $\bar x=13.4$ years |  |  |  |  
**Standard deviation** | $s_x=1.02$ years |  |  |  |  

Assume that all conditions for inference are met.

**Which of the following is a $90\%$ confidence interval for the mean lifetime (in years) for this type of bulb?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###Conditions and formula 

The problem said to assume that all conditions for inference are met (which they are), so we'll make a $t$ interval to estimate the mean.

[[☃ explanation 3]]

[[☃ explanation 4]]

Here's the formula for a one-sample $t$ interval for a mean:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm \left({\text{critical}\atop\text{value}}\right)\left({\text{standard deviation} \atop\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\bar x &\pm\ t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n} 
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=13.4$ years.

Our sample size is $n=5$ bulbs.

Our sample standard deviation is $s_x=1.02$ years.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=5-1=4$, so for $90\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=2.132$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Computation

$\begin{align}
\bar x &\pm t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n}  \\\\
13.4 &\pm 2.132\left(\dfrac{1.02}{\sqrt{5}}\right)
\end{align}$

###Answer

$13.4 \pm 2.132\left(\dfrac{1.02}{\sqrt{5}}\right)$

assessment_items.question_content:
Juana is interested in estimating how long it takes students at her school to complete a maze she designed. She takes a random sample of $4$ students and records how long it takes each of them to complete the maze. They work separately from each other, so Juana is willing to assume independence. Here are the data with summary statistics:

Player | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ | $4$
-: | -: | -: | -: | -: 
**Time (minutes)** | $49$ | $28$ | $37$ | $42$
**Mean** | $\bar x=39$ seconds|  |  |
**Standard deviation** | $s_x=8.83$ seconds|  |  |

Assume that all conditions for inference are met for this sample.

**Which of the following is a $95\%$ confidence interval for the mean time (in seconds) it takes students to complete this maze?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###Conditions and formula 

The problem said to assume that all conditions for inference are met (which they are), so we'll make a $t$ interval to estimate the mean.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

Here's the formula for a one-sample $t$ interval for a mean:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm \left({\text{critical}\atop\text{value}}\right)\left({\text{standard deviation} \atop\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\bar x &\pm\ t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n} 
\end{align}$

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=39$ seconds.

Our sample size is $n=4$ students.

Our sample standard deviation is $s_x=8.83$ seconds.

Our degrees of freedom is $\text{df}=4-1=3$, so for $95\%$ confidence our critical value is $t^{*}=3.182$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Computation

$\begin{align}
\bar x &\pm t^{*} \cdot \dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt n}  \\\\
39 &\pm 3.182\left(\dfrac{8.83}{\sqrt{4}}\right)
\end{align}$

###Answer

$39 \pm 3.182\left(\dfrac{8.83}{\sqrt{4}}\right)$
